---
title: "Session 9 · Nonlinear Relationships"
---

## Overview

Real relationships rarely follow a straight line, yet our model is called *linear*. This session resolves the apparent tension: by adding polynomial and transformed terms, we capture curvature while the model stays linear in its coefficients. It is a striking extension of the "specific → general" arc --- the same estimation machinery now bends to fit U-shapes, diminishing returns, and other curves, without abandoning the regression framework we have built.

## Learning objectives

By the end of this session, you will be able to:

- Diagnose when a straight-line fit fails to capture curvature in the data.
- Add polynomial terms (e.g., a squared predictor) to model a nonlinear relationship.
- Apply variable transformations, such as logs, to linearize a relationship.
- Distinguish "linear in the parameters" from "linear in the predictors."
- Interpret coefficients and fitted curves from a model with nonlinear terms.

## This session's materials

- {{< fa book-open-reader >}} **Read:** [Chapter 6: Nonlinear Relationships](/notes/chapters/06-nonlinear-relationships.html){target="_blank"}
- {{< fa laptop-code >}} **Lab:** [Lab 7: Nonlinear Relationships](/labs/labs/lab07-model-building.html){target="_blank"}
- {{< fa pencil >}} **Homework due:** [Homework 6: Two-Way ANOVA](/assignment/hw06.html)
- {{< fa circle-question >}} **Practice:** [Practice Quiz 6](/practice/chapters/quiz6-two-way-anova.html){target="_blank"}
- {{< fa circle-check >}} **Graded quiz:** [Module Quiz 6](/assignment/quizzes.html#quiz-6)
- {{< fa clipboard-list >}} **Project:** [Project 1 — Draft due](/assignment/project-1-draft.html)

## Key concepts

| Concept | Description |
|---------|-------------|
| **Nonlinear relationship** | An association between predictor and outcome that curves rather than following a straight line. |
| **Polynomial term** | A power of a predictor, such as a squared term, added to a model to capture curvature. |
| **Linear in the parameters** | The property that the model is a linear combination of its coefficients, even when the predictors are transformed. |
| **Variable transformation** | A function, such as a log or square root, applied to a variable to linearize a relationship. |
| **Log transformation** | Taking the logarithm of a variable, often used to model multiplicative or diminishing-returns effects. |
| **Curvature** | The bending in a fitted relationship that a straight line cannot represent. |
| **Fitted curve** | The estimated nonlinear function relating the predictor to the expected outcome. |
